subject: Rename one private local variable in helper.py (single call site)

claims.text: helper.py is described as a private helper with exactly one call site.
claims.disconfirming_observation: Reading the actual file reveals additional call sites, a shadowed name, or that the target variable is not local/private.

claims.text: A private local variable with a single call site has a rename blast radius confined to the helper and that one call site, so the change is reversible and low-risk.
claims.disconfirming_observation: The variable name collides with, or is referenced by, code outside the two shown locations (e.g. string keys, reflection, closures), widening the actual scope.

claims.text: The available source is a one-line description of helper.py, not the live file contents; the variable name and its uses have not been read.
The rename is low stakes: a private identifier change is text-local, non-destructive, and trivially reversible.

control_reason: I am working from a summary of helper.py, not its contents (c3). Before trusting the private/single-call-site framing (c1, c2), the honest next step is to read the actual helper and call site — a read-only, reversible probe that confirms the real scope. The rename itself is low-risk and reversible, but grounding the scope claim requires contacting the live file rather than acting on the summary.
action: Read helper.py and its call site to confirm the variable is truly local, private, and referenced only at the one shown site; then apply the rename at both locations and verify no other reference remains.
residual_uncertainty: The actual variable name and its real number of references are unknown until the file is read.
Whether the name is used indirectly (string key, reflection, re-export) cannot be ruled out from the summary.
'Private' and 'one call site' are asserted by the artifact description, not yet observed in code.
